description:
Goal: the semantic loop should not spend time analyzing impossible activations that never survive into the closed frontier. Signal: the Compiler2 quicksort trace-summary reports zero dead transient activations (`qsort([])`, `qsort(none)`, `append(none, ...)`, `dbg(none)`-style islands), and `CheckSemanticClosure` / `AnalyzeActivation` waste budgets fall materially without changing the final frontier. Strategy: make activation admission and call propagation respect inhabitation and subsumption at the Compiler2 boundary, then tighten the root walk so only live frontier changes reopen semantic work.

Change:
- Revisit activation-key admission and propagation so bottom/empty transient shapes do not become ordinary semantic work unless semantically real.
- Tighten `CheckSemanticClosure` / `AnalyzeActivation` interaction around changed inputs and live waits only.
- Preserve the current closed quicksort and `Enum.reduce` frontiers while reducing rerun churn.

Why:
- The current trace shows real work being spent on dead-end semantic islands that do not survive into the settled root closure.

Checks:
- Quicksort trace-summary reports zero analyzed activations outside the final closure.
- Quicksort trace-summary job-family counts and elapsed totals fall to the expected budget for the rooted quicksort case.
- The final closed activation/executable frontier remains unchanged.
title: Compiler2 fixpoint: prune dead transient activations and reduce root-walk churn
